cards.front: What does port security do, and on which port types is it supported?
cards.back: Limits which MAC addresses can use a switch port (and how many). Supported on access ports and trunks (and host-mode ports). NOT supported on SPAN, dynamic auto/desirable, or EtherChannel members.

cards.front: Configure port security on Fa0/1 to allow up to 2 MACs, learned dynamically, sticky-saved, and shutdown on violation.
cards.back:
```
interface fa0/1
 switchport mode access     ! prerequisite
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky
 switchport port-security violation shutdown
```

cards.front: What are the 3 port-security violation modes and what does each do?
cards.back: shutdown (default): err-disable port, log syslog, increment counter. restrict: drop offending frames, log syslog, increment counter — port stays up. protect: drop offending frames silently, no log, no counter increment, port stays up.

cards.front: What's the difference between dynamic, sticky, and static MAC learning in port security?
cards.back: Dynamic: switch learns MACs into RAM only — lost on reboot. Sticky: dynamically learn AND save to running-config (so they survive reboot if you `wr mem`). Static: manually configured `switchport port-security mac-address AAAA.BBBB.CCCC` — never learned, never aged.

cards.front: Port is err-disabled — how do you bring it back?
cards.back: Manual: `shutdown` then `no shutdown` on the interface. Automatic: enable err-disable recovery globally — `errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation` and `errdisable recovery interval 300` (auto-recover after 5 min).

cards.front: How do you verify port security state and learned MACs?
cards.back: `show port-security` (summary across all ports). `show port-security interface fa0/1` (detailed: mode, max, current count, violations, action). `show port-security address` (every secured MAC).

cards.front: Why does port security require the port to be in 'access' mode?
cards.back: By design, port security applies per-port and per-VLAN. Trunk ports also support it but require additional `switchport port-security maximum X vlan Y` syntax. The simplest scenario is `switchport mode access` + port-security commands. Default `dynamic auto` mode is incompatible.

cards.front: Port-security violation modes truth table: which (a) disables interface, (b) increments counter, (c) sends syslog/SNMP?
cards.back: shutdown (default): disables interface YES, counter YES, syslog YES. restrict: disables NO, counter YES, syslog YES. protect: disables NO, counter NO, syslog NO (silently drops violating frames). Only shutdown actually err-disables; the other two leave the port up and just drop offending traffic.

cards.front: Port-security aging types: absolute vs inactivity, and default behaviors?
cards.back: Absolute (default aging type if any): MAC entry expires N minutes after being learned, regardless of activity. Inactivity: entry expires only after N minutes of no traffic from that MAC. Aging is configured with `switchport port-security aging time <minutes>` and `... aging type {absolute|inactivity}`. Static secure-MAC aging is DISABLED by default — enable it with `... aging static`.

cards.front: What switchport modes is port-security INCOMPATIBLE with?
cards.back: Cannot be enabled on `switchport mode dynamic auto` or `dynamic desirable` — i.e., any port that's still negotiating trunking via DTP. You must hard-code the port to `switchport mode access` (or `switchport mode trunk`) before applying `switchport port-security`.
